entries.title: About the Course
entries.content: The focus of the course in this rotation is on answering the question, "Why do buildings stand up?"  The main text of that name offers the history of great monuments and the architectural technology that makes them possible. Famous buildings from 1800-1900 and 100 BC-350 AD are mentioned as well.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content: Suitable for Grade 8 students with minimal adjustment if combining.

rows.label: Architecture: Grades 9-12
rows.books: Why Buildings Stand Up||50 Buildings You Should Know

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

blocks.text: Connect back to the previous lesson. Take a minute (without looking ideally) to recall the ideas you read about last week.
Look at pictures from links or in the text of design features and/or famous buildings.
Various ways to narrate are assigned in the lessons. Because architecture is an especially visual subject, try to incorporate sketches frequently into your narrations and thoughts.
Discuss what you have learned and found interesting with a parent, teacher, or another student.
Plan time at the end of lesson or later in the afternoon to make additional drawings of buildings or features in a sketchbook and/or Book of Centuries.

These lessons are written directly to the student, but to assist them throughout the year, please read through this section and the lesson plans so you are familiar with the instructions and flow of the lessons. Accountability components are added to facilitate teacher/student discussion and growing independence. 

It is valuable for those in a classroom setting to also have a mix of lessons that students complete silently themselves and others read aloud and narrated together. Because of the visual elements of the course, it is especially suited to completing as a group. Classroom settings lend themselves well to additional discussion and sharing at the end of lessons after everyone has narrated either together or independently with their notebooks.

lessons.title: 30m Architecture: Grades 9-12 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Why Buildings Stand Up, notebook

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
p.17-26

➜WRITE: Copy any diagrams or sketches that would be helpful to you in your notebook. Complete a written narration and be sure to engage with the author's ideas about structure and function in architecture.

lessons.title: 30m Architecture: Grades 9-12 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Why Buildings Stand Up, notebook

➜ READ
~9 pages or what you can read in 20 min

➜NARRATE: Copy any diagrams or sketches that are helpful. Narrate silently and then write about a specific section or idea from the reading.
